Mathematical Details of the MPCurver CAVI Algorithm

cavi_math_details.Rmd
library(MPCurver)

This note is written in the style of supplementary methods. Its purpose is to state the model, define the variational objective, and then derive the explicit update equations implemented by cavi().

Throughout, we focus on the current recommended single-ordering CAVI model used in MPCurver.

We use the following notation throughout:

C:=(c1,…,cn),U:=(u1,…,ud),θ:=(π,σ2,λ), C := (c_1,\dots,c_n), \qquad U := (u_1,\dots,u_d), \qquad \theta := (\pi,\sigma^2,\lambda), where σ2:=(σ12,…,σd2),λ:=(λ1,…,λd). \sigma^2 := (\sigma_1^2,\dots,\sigma_d^2), \qquad \lambda := (\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_d).

We write qq for the full variational distribution q(C,U)q(C,U), rikr_{ik} for the variational responsibility of cell ii for component kk, and rQ:=rank(QK)r_Q := \operatorname{rank}(Q_K) for the rank of the random-walk precision.

1 Model setup

We observe a matrix X=(xij)∈ℝn×d, X = (x_{ij}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}, with cells indexed by i=1,…,ni = 1,\dots,n and features indexed by j=1,…,dj = 1,\dots,d.

1.1 Latent positions

Each cell is assigned to one of KK ordered mixture components: ci∈{1,…,K},Pr(ci=k)=πk,∑k=1Kπk=1. c_i \in \{1,\dots,K\}, \qquad \Pr(c_i = k) = \pi_k, \qquad \sum_{k=1}^K \pi_k = 1.

1.2 Feature trajectories

For each feature jj, we introduce a latent trajectory uj=(uj1,…,ujK)⊤∈ℝK, u_j = (u_{j1},\dots,u_{jK})^\top \in \mathbb{R}^K, where ujku_{jk} is the mean of feature jj at component kk.

Conditionally on ci=kc_i = k, the observation model is xij∣ci=k,uj,σj2∼N(ujk,σj2). x_{ij} \mid c_i = k, u_j, \sigma_j^2 \sim N(u_{jk}, \sigma_j^2).

The current CAVI implementation uses feature-specific variances σj2\sigma_j^2, shared across components kk.

1.3 GMRF prior

Each trajectory is regularized by an intrinsic Gaussian Markov random field prior: uj∼N(0,(λjQK)−), u_j \sim N\!\left(0, (\lambda_j Q_K)^{-}\right), where:

  • QKQ_K is the K×KK \times K random-walk precision matrix,
  • λj>0\lambda_j > 0 is the feature-specific smoothness parameter,
  • (⋅)−(\cdot)^{-} denotes the generalized inverse because QKQ_K is rank-deficient for intrinsic random-walk priors.

Let rQ:=rank(QK)=K−q, r_Q := \operatorname{rank}(Q_K) = K - q, where qq is the random-walk order.

Up to additive constants, the prior density contributes logp(uj∣λj)=rQ2logλj+12log|QK|+−λj2uj⊤QKuj, \log p(u_j \mid \lambda_j) = \frac{r_Q}{2}\log \lambda_j + \frac{1}{2}\log |Q_K|_+ - \frac{\lambda_j}{2} u_j^\top Q_K u_j, where |QK|+|Q_K|_+ is the generalized determinant over the nonzero eigenvalues.

The current package also supports an optional penalty on λj\lambda_j through the induced prior τj:=λj−1/2∼Exp(ρ),ρ≥0. \tau_j := \lambda_j^{-1/2} \sim \operatorname{Exp}(\rho), \qquad \rho \ge 0. Interpreted as a prior on λj\lambda_j, this contributes logp(λj)=log(ρ/2)−32logλj−ρλj−1/2, \log p(\lambda_j) = \log(\rho/2) - \frac{3}{2}\log \lambda_j - \rho \lambda_j^{-1/2}, when ρ>0\rho > 0. The default setting ρ=0\rho = 0 recovers the original unpenalized CAVI objective.

1.4 Joint distribution

The complete-data model factorizes as p(X,C,U∣θ)=[∏i=1np(ci∣π)][∏j=1dp(uj∣λj)][∏i=1n∏j=1dp(xij∣ci,uj,σj2)]. p(X,C,U \mid \theta) = \left[\prod_{i=1}^n p(c_i \mid \pi)\right] \left[\prod_{j=1}^d p(u_j \mid \lambda_j)\right] \left[\prod_{i=1}^n \prod_{j=1}^d p(x_{ij} \mid c_i, u_j, \sigma_j^2)\right].

More explicitly, logp(X,C,U∣θ)=∑i=1nlogπci−12∑i=1n∑j=1d[log(2πσj2)+(xij−uj,ci)2σj2]+∑j=1d[rQ2logλj+12log|QK|+−λj2uj⊤QKuj]+const. \log p(X,C,U \mid \theta) = \sum_{i=1}^n \log \pi_{c_i} - \frac12 \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^d \left[ \log(2\pi \sigma_j^2) + \frac{(x_{ij} - u_{j,c_i})^2}{\sigma_j^2} \right] + \sum_{j=1}^d \left[ \frac{r_Q}{2}\log \lambda_j + \frac{1}{2}\log |Q_K|_+ - \frac{\lambda_j}{2} u_j^\top Q_K u_j \right] + \text{const}.

2 General CAVI idea

We approximate the posterior with the mean-field family q(C,U)=∏i=1nqi(ci)∏j=1dqj(uj). q(C,U) = \prod_{i=1}^n q_i(c_i)\prod_{j=1}^d q_j(u_j).

Write rik:=qi(ci=k),∑k=1Krik=1. r_{ik} := q_i(c_i = k), \qquad \sum_{k=1}^K r_{ik} = 1.

For the trajectory factors we use Gaussians: qj(uj)=N(mj,Sj), q_j(u_j) = N(m_j, S_j), with posterior mean mj∈ℝKm_j \in \mathbb{R}^K and covariance Sj∈ℝK×KS_j \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times K}.

The parameters π\pi, σj2\sigma_j^2, and λj\lambda_j are treated as point-estimated blocks rather than random variational factors.

2.1 ELBO in expectation form

The variational objective is the evidence lower bound (ELBO), ELBO(q;θ)=𝔼q[logp(X,C,U∣θ)]−𝔼q[logq(C,U)]. \operatorname{ELBO}(q;\theta) = \mathbb E_q \big[\log p(X,C,U \mid \theta)\big] - \mathbb E_q \big[\log q(C,U)\big].

For fixed θ\theta, this is a lower bound on the marginal log-evidence logp(X∣θ)=log∫∑Cp(X,C,U∣θ)dU. \log p(X \mid \theta) = \log \int \sum_C p(X,C,U \mid \theta)\, dU. More precisely, logp(X∣θ)=ELBO(q;θ)+KL(q(C,U)∥p(C,U∣X,θ)). \log p(X \mid \theta) = \operatorname{ELBO}(q;\theta) + \mathrm{KL}\!\left( q(C,U)\,\|\,p(C,U \mid X,\theta) \right). So maximizing the ELBO over qq for fixed hyperparameters is equivalent to driving the variational approximation toward the exact posterior.

Once qq has been updated, we then maximize the same ELBO with respect to the point-estimated block θ\theta. This is a standard variational EM / variational empirical-Bayes viewpoint:

  • first optimize the variational approximation qq,
  • then optimize the ELBO as a surrogate for logp(X∣θ)\log p(X \mid \theta),
  • hoping that when the KL gap is small, the optimized ELBO is close to the true marginal log-likelihood.

Equivalently, if we define the profiled variational objective F(θ):=maxqELBO(q;θ), F(\theta) := \max_q \operatorname{ELBO}(q;\theta), then F(θ)≤logp(X∣θ). F(\theta) \le \log p(X \mid \theta). So at a high level the algorithm alternates between:

  1. updating the variational approximation qq to improve the bound,
  2. then updating θ\theta to increase the optimized bound FF.

This is why the procedure can be viewed as “approximately maximizing the marginal log-likelihood”: we optimize a lower bound to it, rather than the intractable quantity directly.

Substituting the model terms into the ELBO definition gives ELBO(q;θ)=𝔼q[logp(C∣π)]+𝔼q[logp(X∣C,U,σ2)]+𝔼q[logp(U∣λ)]+H(qC)+H(qU), \operatorname{ELBO}(q;\theta) = \mathbb E_q[\log p(C \mid \pi)] + \mathbb E_q[\log p(X \mid C,U,\sigma^2)] + \mathbb E_q[\log p(U \mid \lambda)] + H(q_C) + H(q_U), where H(⋅)H(\cdot) denotes entropy.

The major coordinate-ascent idea is:

  1. fix all blocks except one,
  2. maximize the ELBO with respect to that block,
  3. cycle until convergence.

For the variational factors, the generic mean-field update is logqℓ⋆(ξℓ)=𝔼q−ℓ[logp(X,C,U∣θ)]+const, \log q_\ell^\star(\xi_\ell) = \mathbb E_{q_{-\ell}}[\log p(X,C,U \mid \theta)] + \text{const}, where ξℓ\xi_\ell denotes the argument of the current variational block. In the present model this means:

  • ξℓ=ci\xi_\ell = c_i when the current block is the categorical factor qi(ci)q_i(c_i),
  • ξℓ=uj\xi_\ell = u_j when the current block is the Gaussian factor qj(uj)q_j(u_j).

Here q−ℓq_{-\ell} means “all variational factors except the current one”.

For the point-estimated parameter block θ\theta, we maximize the same ELBO directly with respect to that parameter block.

3 Expanded ELBO

To derive the code formulas, define the effective component sizes Nk:=∑i=1nrik. N_k := \sum_{i=1}^n r_{ik}.

Then the mixture-weight term is 𝔼q[logp(C∣π)]=∑i=1n∑k=1Kriklogπk. \mathbb E_q[\log p(C \mid \pi)] = \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{k=1}^K r_{ik}\log \pi_k.

The entropy of the categorical factors is H(qC)=−∑i=1n∑k=1Kriklogrik. H(q_C) = - \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{k=1}^K r_{ik}\log r_{ik}.

For the Gaussian likelihood, 𝔼q[logp(X∣C,U,σ2)]=−12∑i=1n∑k=1K∑j=1drik[log(2πσj2)+𝔼q[(xij−ujk)2]σj2]. \mathbb E_q[\log p(X \mid C,U,\sigma^2)] = -\frac12 \sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{k=1}^K\sum_{j=1}^d r_{ik} \left[ \log(2\pi \sigma_j^2) + \frac{\mathbb E_q[(x_{ij} - u_{jk})^2]}{\sigma_j^2} \right].

Since qj(uj)=N(mj,Sj)q_j(u_j)=N(m_j,S_j), 𝔼q[(xij−ujk)2]=(xij−mjk)2+Sj,kk. \mathbb E_q[(x_{ij} - u_{jk})^2] = (x_{ij} - m_{jk})^2 + S_{j,kk}.

So the likelihood contribution becomes −12∑i=1n∑k=1K∑j=1drik[log(2πσj2)+(xij−mjk)2+Sj,kkσj2]. -\frac12 \sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{k=1}^K\sum_{j=1}^d r_{ik} \left[ \log(2\pi \sigma_j^2) + \frac{(x_{ij} - m_{jk})^2 + S_{j,kk}}{\sigma_j^2} \right].

For the prior, 𝔼q[logp(U∣λ)]=12∑j=1d[rQlogλj+log|QK|+−λj𝔼q(uj⊤QKuj)]. \mathbb E_q[\log p(U \mid \lambda)] = \frac12 \sum_{j=1}^d \left[ r_Q \log \lambda_j + \log |Q_K|_+ - \lambda_j \mathbb E_q(u_j^\top Q_K u_j) \right].

Using Gaussian second moments, 𝔼q(uj⊤QKuj)=mj⊤QKmj+tr(QKSj). \mathbb E_q(u_j^\top Q_K u_j) = m_j^\top Q_K m_j + \operatorname{tr}(Q_K S_j).

Finally, the entropy of qj(uj)=N(mj,Sj)q_j(u_j)=N(m_j,S_j) is H(qj)=12[K(1+log2π)+log|Sj|], H(q_j) = \frac12 \left[K(1+\log 2\pi) + \log |S_j|\right], so H(qU)=12∑j=1d[K(1+log2π)+log|Sj|]. H(q_U) = \frac12 \sum_{j=1}^d \left[K(1+\log 2\pi) + \log |S_j|\right].

Putting everything together, ELBO(q;θ)=∑i=1n∑k=1Kriklogπk−∑i=1n∑k=1Kriklogrik \operatorname{ELBO}(q;\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{k=1}^K r_{ik}\log \pi_k - \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{k=1}^K r_{ik}\log r_{ik} −12∑i=1n∑k=1K∑j=1drik[log(2πσj2)+(xij−mjk)2+Sj,kkσj2] -\frac12 \sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{k=1}^K\sum_{j=1}^d r_{ik} \left[ \log(2\pi \sigma_j^2) + \frac{(x_{ij} - m_{jk})^2 + S_{j,kk}}{\sigma_j^2} \right] +12∑j=1d[rQlogλj+log|QK|+−λj(mj⊤QKmj+tr(QKSj))+log|Sj|+K(1+log2π)]. + \frac12 \sum_{j=1}^d \left[ r_Q \log \lambda_j + \log |Q_K|_+ - \lambda_j\left(m_j^\top Q_K m_j + \operatorname{tr}(Q_K S_j)\right) + \log |S_j| + K(1+\log 2\pi) \right].

This expanded form is the starting point for every concrete update in the code.

4 Specific coordinate updates

4.1 Update for qj(uj)q_j(u_j)

Fix rikr_{ik}, σj2\sigma_j^2, and λj\lambda_j, and keep only the ELBO terms that depend on uju_j.

Ignoring constants, we get logqj⋆(uj)=−12σj2∑i=1n∑k=1Krik(xij−ujk)2−λj2uj⊤QKuj+const. \log q_j^\star(u_j) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma_j^2} \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{k=1}^K r_{ik}(x_{ij} - u_{jk})^2 - \frac{\lambda_j}{2} u_j^\top Q_K u_j + \text{const}.

Expanding the quadratic term in uju_j, logqj⋆(uj)=−12uj⊤[λjQK+diag(N1/σj2,…,NK/σj2)]uj+uj⊤bj+const, \log q_j^\star(u_j) = -\frac12 u_j^\top \left[ \lambda_j Q_K + \operatorname{diag}(N_1/\sigma_j^2,\dots,N_K/\sigma_j^2) \right] u_j + u_j^\top b_j + \text{const}, where bjk=1σj2∑i=1nrikxij. b_{jk} = \frac{1}{\sigma_j^2}\sum_{i=1}^n r_{ik} x_{ij}.

Therefore qj⋆(uj)q_j^\star(u_j) is Gaussian with Aj:=λjQK+diag(N1/σj2,…,NK/σj2), A_j := \lambda_j Q_K + \operatorname{diag}(N_1/\sigma_j^2,\dots,N_K/\sigma_j^2), Sj=Aj−1,mj=Sjbj. S_j = A_j^{-1}, \qquad m_j = S_j b_j.

This is exactly the matrix solve used in the implementation.

4.2 Update for qi(ci)q_i(c_i)

Fix q(U)q(U), π\pi, and σj2\sigma_j^2, and focus on the terms involving the single latent variable cic_i.

The general mean-field rule gives logqi⋆(ci=k)∝logπk+𝔼q[logp(xi∣ci=k,U,σ2)]. \log q_i^\star(c_i = k) \propto \log \pi_k + \mathbb E_q[\log p(x_i \mid c_i = k, U, \sigma^2)].

Using the Gaussian likelihood, logqi⋆(ci=k)∝logπk−12∑j=1d[log(2πσj2)+(xij−mjk)2+Sj,kkσj2]. \log q_i^\star(c_i = k) \propto \log \pi_k - \frac12 \sum_{j=1}^d \left[ \log(2\pi \sigma_j^2) + \frac{(x_{ij} - m_{jk})^2 + S_{j,kk}}{\sigma_j^2} \right].

So the update is a row-wise softmax: rik=exp(ℓik)∑k′=1Kexp(ℓik′), r_{ik} = \frac{\exp(\ell_{ik})}{\sum_{k'=1}^K \exp(\ell_{ik'})}, with ℓik:=logπk−12∑j=1d[log(2πσj2)+(xij−mjk)2+Sj,kkσj2]. \ell_{ik} := \log \pi_k - \frac12 \sum_{j=1}^d \left[ \log(2\pi \sigma_j^2) + \frac{(x_{ij} - m_{jk})^2 + S_{j,kk}}{\sigma_j^2} \right].

The posterior variance term Sj,kkS_{j,kk} is the uncertainty correction that distinguishes this update from a pure plug-in EM step.

4.3 Update for πk\pi_k

Holding everything else fixed, maximize ∑i=1n∑k=1Kriklogπk \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{k=1}^K r_{ik}\log \pi_k subject to ∑kπk=1\sum_k \pi_k = 1.

Using a Lagrange multiplier, πk=Nkn. \pi_k = \frac{N_k}{n}.

4.4 Update for σj2\sigma_j^2

Fix q(U)q(U) and q(C)q(C), and keep only the ELBO terms involving σj2\sigma_j^2: −12∑i=1n∑k=1Krik[logσj2+(xij−mjk)2+Sj,kkσj2]. -\frac12 \sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{k=1}^K r_{ik} \left[ \log \sigma_j^2 + \frac{(x_{ij} - m_{jk})^2 + S_{j,kk}}{\sigma_j^2} \right].

Differentiating with respect to σj2\sigma_j^2 and setting the derivative to zero yields σj2=1n∑i=1n∑k=1Krik[(xij−mjk)2+Sj,kk]. \sigma_j^2 = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{k=1}^K r_{ik}\left[(x_{ij} - m_{jk})^2 + S_{j,kk}\right].

In the code this is followed by projection onto the allowed range [sigma_min, sigma_max].

4.5 Update for λj\lambda_j

Fix q(U)q(U), and write qj:=mj⊤QKmj+tr(QKSj). q_j := m_j^\top Q_K m_j + \operatorname{tr}(Q_K S_j).

Without the optional τj∼Exp(ρ)\tau_j \sim \operatorname{Exp}(\rho) penalty, the only ELBO terms involving λj\lambda_j are rQ2logλj−λj2qj. \frac{r_Q}{2}\log \lambda_j - \frac{\lambda_j}{2} q_j.

Differentiating and setting to zero gives the familiar closed-form update λj=rQqj, \lambda_j = \frac{r_Q}{q_j}, followed by projection onto [lambda_min, lambda_max].

With the induced prior active, the λj\lambda_j block becomes rQ−32logλj−λj2qj−ρλj−1/2+log(ρ/2). \frac{r_Q-3}{2}\log \lambda_j - \frac{\lambda_j}{2} q_j - \rho \lambda_j^{-1/2} + \log(\rho/2).

This no longer has a closed-form maximizer in λj\lambda_j. The implementation therefore optimizes the same scalar objective in ηj:=logλj, \eta_j := \log \lambda_j, which yields rQ−32ηj−qj2eηj−ρe−ηj/2+log(ρ/2). \frac{r_Q-3}{2}\eta_j - \frac{q_j}{2} e^{\eta_j} - \rho e^{-\eta_j/2} + \log(\rho/2).

Its derivative is rQ−32−qj2eηj+ρ2e−ηj/2, \frac{r_Q-3}{2} - \frac{q_j}{2} e^{\eta_j} + \frac{\rho}{2} e^{-\eta_j/2}, which is strictly decreasing in ηj\eta_j, so the optimum is unique. In the code this is solved by a bounded one-dimensional root search on log(lambda_j), and the resulting maximizer is then mapped back to [lambda_min, lambda_max].

5 What the code actually stores

The main quantities in the derivation correspond to the following objects in cavi():

Mathematical quantity Code object
rikr_{ik} R[i, k] or final fit$gamma[i, k]
Nk=∑irikN_k = \sum_i r_{ik} Nk <- colSums(R)
mjkm_{jk} q_u$m_mat[j, k] or fit$posterior$mean[j, k]
SjS_j q_u$S_list[[j]] or fit$posterior$cov[[j]]
Sj,kkS_{j,kk} q_u$sdiag_mat[j, k] or fit$posterior$var[j, k]
∑irikxij\sum_i r_{ik} x_{ij} GX[j, k]
mj⊤QKmj+tr(QKSj)m_j^\top Q_K m_j + \operatorname{tr}(Q_K S_j) q_u$eq_quad[j]

So the derivation above is not just conceptual: it is exactly the algebra that drives the implementation.

6 Why this gives a clean monotonicity story

The key point is that all five updates above are exact optimizers of their own ELBO blocks:

  • Gaussian update for qj(uj)q_j(u_j),
  • softmax update for qi(ci)q_i(c_i),
  • normalized counts for πk\pi_k,
  • closed-form posterior residual update for σj2\sigma_j^2,
  • closed-form or one-dimensional concave update for λj\lambda_j, depending on whether the induced λ\lambda prior is active.

Because cavi() cycles through exact coordinate maximizers of the same explicit ELBO, the recorded elbo_trace should be nondecreasing up to numerical tolerance. Importantly, when lambda_sd_prior_rate > 0, the stored elbo_trace includes the full induced-prior contribution as well. That is the core optimization guarantee that makes the current cavi pipeline cleaner than the older hybrid collapsed-EM path.

On this page

Developed by Ziang Zhang.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
